perturbation.original_text: Authorised Person means, in relation to the Recipient, any of the following only to the extent that they are engaged in respect of the Purpose: its officers, directors, employees, and any other person who has been previously approved in writing by the Disclosers
perturbation.changed_text: Authorised Person means, in relation to the Recipient, only its officers and directors to the extent that they are engaged in respect of the Purpose. Any other person, including employees, shall not be considered an Authorized Person under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by strictly limiting 'Authorised Persons' to only officers and directors, explicitly excluding employees and others who previously could be approved in writing. This creates uncertainty because later clauses referring to 'Authorised Persons' will now have a narrower scope, potentially conflicting with scenarios where employees are intended to handle Confidential Information, creating an in-text contradiction in clause 3.1, where it says 'The Recipient may disclose any of the Confidential Information to any of its Authorised Persons'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient may disclose any of the Confidential Information to any of its Authorised Persons, provided that it informs them beforehand of the duties of confidence under this Agreement, ensures that they undertake to the Recipient to comply with the same duties of confidence, keeps a written account of each of the disclosures, advises the Disclosers immediately it becomes aware of any breach by an Authorised Person, and gives upon any request by the Disclosers evidence of compliance with this clause.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly narrows the definition of 'Authorised Person' to only officers and directors, excluding employees. The contradicted text states 'The Recipient may disclose any of the Confidential Information to any of its Authorised Persons'. This creates a direct contradiction because the changed definition now excludes employees, who were potentially considered Authorised Persons under the original text, thus impacting the ability to disclose confidential information to them, which the contradicted text allows.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any information in whatever form (whether in writing, electronic or digital form, verbally or by inspection of documents, computer systems or sites or pursuant to discussions or by any other means ) which is confidential in nature, designated orally or in writing by the Disclosers as confidential or which may reasonably be considered by a business person to be commercially sensitive provided by the Disclosers (directly or indirectly) by any means to the Recipient (or to any Authorised Person)
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by the Disclosers. No other information, regardless of its nature or how it is communicated, will be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is narrowed to include only information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing. This conflicts with the original definition, which included information 'designated orally' or 'which may reasonably be considered by a business person to be commercially sensitive'. This creates an in-text contradiction with Clause 2.1.1 because it mentions 'keep all the Confidential Information secret and confidential, and not disclose any of the Confidential Information to any person other than in accordance with clause 3', which will not be applied correctly due to the new definition of Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: keep all the Confidential Information secret and confidential, and not disclose any of the Confidential Information to any person other than in accordance with clause 3;
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include only explicitly marked information, conflicting directly with the contradicted text, which requires keeping 'all the Confidential Information secret and confidential'. The original definition included information designated orally or considered commercially sensitive, creating a clear contradiction because the changed definition no longer includes that information, rendering the obligation in the contradicted text unenforceable for information not explicitly marked as confidential.

perturbation.changed_text: Purpose Franchise agreement, but only if the Recipient adheres to the terms outlined in Schedule A.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'but only if the Recipient adheres to the terms outlined in Schedule A' to the definition of Purpose, this creates a condition for the Franchise agreement that's not explicitly detailed elsewhere in the agreement itself (since Schedule A doesn't exist). This introduces an in-text contradiction because Clause 2.1 implies the Disclosers may disclose Confidential Information 'for the Purpose', but that purpose is now conditional and undefined, leading to uncertainty about when the disclosure is permitted, because there is no Schedule A. It will introduce an in-text contradiction in clause 2.1 where it states 'The Disclosers may (but are not obliged to) disclose during the term of this Agreement, and/or may have already disclosed, Confidential Information to the Recipient for the Purpose'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Disclosers may (but are not obliged to) disclose during the term of this Agreement, and/or may have already disclosed, Confidential Information to the Recipient for the Purpose and, in consideration of the Disclosers disclosing any Confidential Information to the Recipient and the payment to the Recipient of £1 (one pound) the receipt of which is acknowledged by the Recipient), the Recipient undertakes to the Disclosers that it shall, and shall procure that its Authorised Persons shall:
perturbation.justification: The original text simply defined the 'Purpose Franchise agreement'. The changed text adds a condition: 'but only if the Recipient adheres to the terms outlined in Schedule A.' This condition is problematic because there's no Schedule A, as stated in the explanation. This creates an in-text contradiction with the contradicted text, which states 'The Disclosers may... disclose... Confidential Information to the Recipient for the Purpose...' because the 'Purpose' is now conditional and undefined due to the missing Schedule A, leading to uncertainty about when the disclosure is permitted. The changed text, therefore, introduces a direct inconsistency according to the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosers from and against all and any losses, damages (including any consequential, direct, indirect, special, incidental or punitive damages or loss or any other form of economic loss), liability, costs (including legal fees), expenses, claims (including any settlements of claims before or after issue of proceedings), actions, proceedings, judgment sums (including sums arising from consent orders or judgments) fines and penalties, however arising out of, or in connection with, the breach of this Agreement or the unauthorised disclosure or Use of any of the Confidential Information by the Recipient or by any of its Authorised Persons.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosers only for direct damages proven to be a direct result of a deliberate and malicious breach of this Agreement by the Recipient. No other forms of damages or losses are covered under this indemnity.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly narrows the scope of the indemnity. It now only covers 'direct damages' resulting from a 'deliberate and malicious breach', removing coverage for consequential, indirect, or incidental damages, and breaches that aren't 'deliberate and malicious'. It creates an in-text contradiction when considering the Equitable Relief clause (7), which states that breaches may cause irreparable harm. The narrowed indemnity might not provide adequate compensation for 'irreparable harm,' thus undermining the purpose of equitable relief. So the contradiction is between the limited indemnity coverage and the potential for substantial harm.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient recognises that any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement may cause the Disclosers irreparable harm for which damages may not be an adequate remedy. Accordingly, in addition to any other remedies and damages available to the Disclosers, the Recipient acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosers is entitled to the remedies of specific performance, injunction and other equitable relief without proof of special damages.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly limits the scope of the indemnity to only 'direct damages' resulting from 'deliberate and malicious breach', while the contradicted text states that breaches may cause 'irreparable harm for which damages may not be an adequate remedy' and allows for equitable relief. The limited indemnity contradicts the possibility of 'irreparable harm' that may not be adequately covered by direct damages, thus creating a conflict.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt by the Recipient of a written demand from the Disclosers: 8.1.1 the Recipient must return or procure the return to the Disclosers or, as the Disclosers may require, destroy or procure the destruction of any and all materials containing the Confidential Information together with all copies; 8.1.2 if the Disclosers requires, the Recipient must provide the Disclosers with a certificate or such other evidence as the Disclosers may reasonably require duly signed or executed by an officer of the Recipient confirming that the Recipient has complied with all of its obligations under this Agreement including about return, destruction and deletion of Confidential Information and media; 8.1.3 the Recipient must delete or procure the deletion of all electronic copies of Confidential Information; and 8.1.4 the Recipient must make, and procure that the Authorised Persons shall make, no further Use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, regardless of the reason, the Recipient is only required to cease using the Confidential Information. The Recipient is NOT required to return, destroy, or delete any materials containing Confidential Information. The Recipient is also not required to provide any certificate confirming compliance.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation directly contradicts the original 'Delivery Up' clause. The Recipient is now explicitly NOT required to return, destroy, or delete any Confidential Information upon termination, while the original clause mandates these actions upon written demand. This directly contradicts the first sentence of the 'Delivery Up' clause, creating a clear conflict in the obligations of the Recipient upon termination. This will make the first sentence of this very same clause an in-text contradiction because this will be directly contradicting.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon receipt by the Recipient of a written demand from the Disclosers:
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that upon termination, the Recipient is NOT required to return, destroy, or delete any materials, while the contradicted text (which is the first part of the original clause) states that upon receipt of a written demand, the Recipient must return or destroy materials. This creates a direct conflict in the obligations of the Recipient, forming a strong in-text contradiction as described in the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: No variation of this Agreement shall be valid or effective unless it is in writing, refers to this Agreement and is duly signed or executed by, or on behalf of, each party.
perturbation.changed_text: Variations to this agreement can be made verbally if both parties agree. A written record is preferred but not required for the variation to be valid.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by allowing verbal variations of the agreement, directly conflicting with the original clause that requires all variations to be in writing and signed. This creates ambiguity on how the agreement can be changed and if those changes are enforcable. This modification creates an in-text contradiction to Clause 9.1, which specifies that the entire agreement is contained within this document. Verbal agreements would therefore not be included, and would thus be contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties agree that this Agreement and any documents entered into pursuant to it constitute the entire agreement between them and supersedes all previous agreements, understandings and arrangements between them, whether in writing or oral in respect of its subject matter.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction to the contradicted text. The original text strictly prohibits verbal variations to the agreement, requiring written and signed modifications. The changed text explicitly allows for verbal variations, creating a direct conflict. The contradicted text, states that the agreement and its documents is the 'entire agreement' superceding anything oral, creating a strong in-text contradiction that falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'. The modified section makes the agreement unclear and unenforceable.
